And because these were forced upon us and then salmon on top of that, they were shutting down all the industries. The environmentalists at the same time were appealing timber sales and suing over everything. And it was causing a lot of communities to just dry up and go away when they'd lose their mills. Council's a perfect example of that council. Idaho, that used to be a thriving community, absolutely thriving. They had a Boise Cascade mill there. There were lots and lots of businesses. Logging supported the whole place. Logging and milling. Great paying jobs. And finally, the environmentalists won out. There was no more timber sales. And so Boise Cascade sold their mill to Mexico. I so, uh, early on in Idaho's history or the West history, the feds get involved with wolf eradication roughly around 1915. Uh, it was happening before that at the state level when it was a, you know, territorial states were offering bounties and fur pelts and so on and so forth, mostly to protect, you know, uh, immigrants traveling on the on various trails, but also cattle. Yes. So what changed in your mind? You know, this this. I'm not a big believer. Just I haven't read enough, I guess, to convince me. But the the typical explanation is a change in ecological thinking that happens in the 60s. You have Silent Spring. You know Rachel Carson. Um, but it always seems like there should be a more monetary or material explanation for the the federal government's change in wanting to do restoration work or, you know, uh, prevent logging or something like that. Does do you have a thought about why? What changed? Well, in the 70s, the early 70s, that's when the EPA came into existence. That's the Clean Water Act, the Clean Air Act, the Endangered Species Act. Right. Everything just kind of came together all at once. And when I was working for Helen, I remember one time I was back there and the Sierra Club was holding a rally on the steps. So a staffer and I went to it, and they had huge pictures of clearcuts from the 50s. We could tell it was a 50s because of the equipment in the background. And they said, this is what's going on in the West today. They just flat lie. And people in the East don't know the difference. They really don't. They don't have a clue. Not a clue in the world. Most of them have never been west of the Mississippi. So Helen decided we needed to do show me tours. So we brought some of the high level staffers from those agencies out here. And Dale Bosworth helped me with this because then he was the regional forester for the region. Up north of the salmon River, Idaho split in two different regions. So he moved from Ogden up to Missoula. So he helped me with his tour and we took them up on we call it the 500 road. It's the Nez Perce Trail. It's where Lewis and Clark came over the mountain. And it's where they saw the Sea of Mountains. That was where they had to killed Colt Creek, where they had to kill their horses to eat because there was nothing. There were no deer, elk, anything up in there. And they were very, very discouraged and starving. So we took these people up there on top of that. And I said, now you can see we have not leveled every mountain, we've not cut every tree, and we've not polluted every stream. And these people had never been west of the Mississippi. And these are mostly congressmen or. No, these are bureaucrats. These are DC, EPA, U.S. Fish and Wildlife, all the agency bureaucrats under the Clinton administration. What was the reaction? They were just flabbergasted. They had never had any idea. These were the people making the decisions for the West. Yeah. And then I reminded them the first time I went to the East and looked at the Potomac River, I was horrified that they were telling us how to deal with our crystal clean water when theirs was polluted. So did anything change as a result of them. Seeing some of the things did change for a little while, and then those bureaucrats, you know, moved on. Yeah. So what can you think of anything that did change? For were they more responsive to. They were more responsive to the congressman. Okay. And back then this is another thing. Back then Congress had a lot more control over the agencies because they had the power of the purse. They did appropriation bills every single year. And so they had hearings and they would bring in the directors and the administrators, and they would say, this is happening in my district. What do you have to say of this? Why should we give you money to continue harming our people? But then after nine over 11, none of that went on anymore. It was all just continuing resolutions. You get the same amount plus a percent for the next year. And so they had no power over the bureaucrats any longer. So that has changed recently with, uh, Supreme. Yes. No. We hope. Yeah. We'll see. I mean, I don't know how much the rule making process factored into this, but it seems like it did so well. So they're classified under Essa. Essa's 73. Something to that. Effect. Somewhere around there. I can't remember exactly when. So there's wolves reintroduced into Yellowstone, I think in 78 or 9. Um, and then I told that there are reports of wolves and some old Phil Davis, I think, was one person who said, you know, there's always been wolves. We've always seen wolves. They were just smaller and different, you know, smaller populations stayed away from most folks. Um, so, you know, the, the, I guess, the role of the EPA in environmental legislation, you know, ESA and stuff like that. Do you think that, uh, set well with, you know, your basic idahoan or your general idahoan in the sense of like, we are restoring, you know, wilderness is an example. Uh, or, you know, was it seen as kind of this is another, maybe another imposition that's being used, uh, in ways that we are not in agreement. Against us, forcing us. Idahoans don't like to be forced. Yeah, you can suggest thing to Idahoans, but you can't say you will do this because they're going to say, no, we're not. I think there's a lot of maybe Irishmen around here. Well, I guess, you know, I'm curious too, between about. Excuse me. Um, just there's in my mind there's several constituencies. Obviously, ranching community is very much opposed and, uh, more interested in resource management on their own terms. Uh, what about the average, you know, average Boise citizen? Uh, what about people in cities, uh, folks who are not involved in resource extraction? Did you get a sense of what the general attitude of the state was at that time? Um, the hunters were mostly against it because they'd figured out these wolves were going to compete against them. When things really changes, when wolves started running the elk into the homes or into the yards over at Sun Valley and killing them in the winter. That woke some of those people up over there. And then animals started disappearing down here. They were seeing wolves in the foothills. Um, there were people who would go hiking up in the in the mountains with their dogs and wolves hate dogs, and they would have their dog killed in front of them. That changed a lot of minds. Was this between 95 and I mean, what. Was later. Later. When wolves were a lot more populated? Early 2000, maybe. Yeah. Okay. Um, sorry. I'm checking our time. Um, we set down three. Okay. Wow. Sorry. The time. It's 410, so I wanted to. 20 minutes. Um, well, I wanted to ask you, you know, now that we're kind of over the hump of wolf reintroduction itself, what was your, general, you know, day to day, like, I guess, in D.C. and not just around wolves, but, uh, you're dealing with wildlands, public lands, other, you know, resource issues, other environmental policy issues. Is there anything that stands out to you during your time with, uh, or Congresswoman Congressman Chenoweth that, you know, aside from wolves, is there anything? Definitely salmon. Salmon took up a lot of my time. Um, it became that Washington and Oregon thought they they needed our water. They tried to take our water to dump into the ocean. It was stupid. And they are still wanting to breach dams, which is stupid. So I had I spent a lot of time down in Portland trying to educate people, trying to understand, Um, I would go to the University of Washington, where the researchers for National Marine Fisheries were the biologists, and they said it won't do any good to breach dams. It's not going to happen. It's in the ocean and it goes in cycles. So some years you're going to have lots of them. Some years you're not going to have many. Yeah. So I learned a lot about that. I fought all the time against the environmentalists who they just use it for fundraiser. They use wolves for a fundraiser. They don't care about the people. So when you say environmentalists, are you referring to large organizations or were there smaller groups like, you know, I assume the state level organizations like the Idaho Conservation League probably don't have as much power as the Sierra Club. So who was the. Oh, they're in cahoots. Believe me. Yeah. The Sierra Club works on Congress and the Idaho Conservation League. Sues to stop everything. As do Idaho. Idaho, rivers United us. Then there are other national groups that do the same thing. And ESA has a citizen lawsuit, as does the Clean Water Act, the Clean Air Act. So those groups can actually sue and they can sue on just on any little tiny thing. So if they do, the agencies do an EIS and they forget to dot an I or cross a T, then the enviros can sue. They win or do a settlement and then they get their attorney fees. Yeah. So these, um, I mean, I know the Sierra Club is a large lots of funding. You know, I have a. uh. I won't say a friend. A friend of a friend. Um, I don't know him personally, but he does. Environmental sociology primarily focused on activism. And his. This is a WSU, uh, his, you know, findings, uh, were, you know, essentially, it's a lot of money coming from outside. There's a lot of groups. And he echoes it pretty much exactly what you just said, that it's a lot, a lot of times it's for funding purposes. Uh, they've never visited there. It's kind of seen as a park for, you know, for very wealthy people in the East Coast, but where their local organizations that were, you know, in favor of wolf reintroduction even all the way up to delisting, I guess, you know, advocating for, uh, for it in the state. Is there any in your mind at the local level? Um, defenders of wildlife? Okay. We're very involved. And what is the other one? The other federation. That's a wildlife. Federation. Okay. But at the legislature, they have no poll whatsoever. Yeah. When the Idaho. Honestly, when the Idaho Conservation League comes and lobbies against a bill, you know it's going to pass. Yeah, without a doubt. Yeah. They have just burnt every bridge ever because of of their extremism. Now, my, uh, research was shown that the Idaho Conservation League stayed away from wolf reintroduction as an issue. Was that what you remember? They were helping quietly into the table. They were letting defenders do most of it. Okay. Okay. So they were pushing for it. So we're let's think chronologically now we're after De-listing. Uh, how has it been since I guess as far as a political issue is, is there still the same level of contention around it at the local level? I know there's been some recent changes in the last few years about hunting. Um, can you speak to your what you've seen at the state level? Um, yes. I wrote most of the of our laws of how to get a handle on wolf reintroduction and getting some kind of control on these critters, and then trying to reimburse ranchers for their enormous losses. Can you speak to, um, some of the specifics about how those losses are calculated? Because I think it's more in depth than people understand. Um, just about from. Well, first you have to when when it first started, you had to have a confirmed loss. That's very, very difficult in the summer up in the mountains. A rancher maybe will find a leg. Well, how are you going to tell what killed that animal? You can't. And again, it was Wildlife Services USDA that had to go. They still do. They have to go and confirm it. So let's say, you know, finally you were lucky enough to find a whole carcass, and then you would get a hold after you got back home that night and you'd call the trapper and he'd say, well, I can't be there for three days. Well, by that time, the Coyotes, the Ravens, you don't have any evidence left. But if for some reason he said, yeah, I'll be right there because we had one trapper that would travel in the middle of the night to get there, to prove it. All right. Then you would send in his findings to our Office of Species Conservation, and then they would put you on a list. And there was a certain amount of money that the federal government sent. Again, that was part of the agreement, and I think it was $100,000. But if there were a lot of losses that were confirmed, you might get $0.05 on the dollar for what that animal was worth. So most ranchers went, what's the point? And are those is the way that that's calculating changing? Because I've spoke to some folks in Wyoming and, you know, their argument is it takes a long time to get an animal to market. And so you're not just compensating me for the loss of that animal. You're compensating me for the years that I've had to put into this animal to grow, to be profitable. Is that. That? Well, that's true. That's what the rancher loses. So if they lose a cow that they have bred in their herd for specific genetics. And then they've lost her. They get to start all over again. They lost her and they lost her calf and any calf she would have had. Let's say she's a two year old. They've lost 7 or 8, nine years worth of calves. It's a huge loss. And wolves, they, they worry the animals so they don't feed like they should. They don't reproduce, they don't breed back. They lose a lot of weight. Their calves come back to the ranch in the fall if you're lucky enough to get them back, and there are a lot lighter than they should have been. So a couple of years ago, I passed a law where now you can have your historical data, you can use it. So what you used to have back, you used to have, let's say, when your calves Cavs came back. They weighed £600. And now when they come back they're weighing £400. So you can be reimbursed for that. But that's state funds not federal. That's interesting. That's uh I hadn't heard that before. So that's. And how recent is that piece of legislation? Um, I think it's two years old. Two years old. Um, but at the time that we have left and, you know, most certainly not putting you on the spot, but love to do it again. Uh, I'll be back later this summer. Uh, and we can expound upon some of the things that we've touched on. Is there anything specifically that you can think of or are particularly proud of? Uh, during your time? I guess we glossed over your director of environmental, uh, policy for Helen Chenoweth. When did you become a representative of your representative yourself? Um, in oh nine. I never wanted to run for office, and Helen always tried to get me to do it. Yeah. And I said, why would I be crazy enough to do that? I see what they do to you. They were hateful to her. They are horrible to her. They lied about her. It was terrible. And it hurt her a lot. But she never showed that. So I don't want anything to do with that. That would be crazy. And she'd say, you have to do that. Well, then Helen was killed in that car wreck. And a couple years later, we had our state representative took a job in the governor's office, so there was an opening. So my job was always go find candidates. So you have to have three names that are sent to the governor. Well, we could only find two conservatives that agreed to do it. And so I was grousing to my former senator about, gosh, I can't find anybody else that wants to do this. And now we have a liberal who thinks she wants to do it, and she doesn't believe in property rights. And he said, Judy, run yourself. And I said, why would I do that? He said, because you have been trained since you were a kid to do this. And it was almost I could hear Helen say, when the door opens, I'm going to kick you through it. So I ran. Okay. Well, you've been you've been in office for. A long time. Yeah, over a decade. Um, so is there anything that you take away from your time? One with Helen, I guess. Even before that, uh, being interested, most people don't go to public meetings, uh, and involve themselves. Now, you've been a representative for some time. Uh, do you have any insight about, you know, public service itself, but then, uh, how policy actually comes into being? How does you know? You write you write a piece of legislation, but there's a difference between what's written and how it's enacted. So do you have any insight? At first, we'll take it in two parts. Your views on public service. And then what? How does how is the sausage made? I guess the sausage is not fun and it tastes really bad. Um, public service should be exactly that. You should be helping your constituents, and you should be voting for bills that help your constituents. So Idaho is very, very diverse, you know. Um, the north end of Boise is totally different than my district. And nobody can expect people to vote exactly alike, all especially on every single bill. So our jobs are to educate each other. So I try to educate the city people about what life is like out there in rural Idaho. And most of the time they're very, very willing to listen. And they try to educate us about urban renewal districts of which I'd never heard before. Some of these other things that are foreign to all of us. Um, so a public servant should be willing to listen not only to their constituents, but to their colleagues. They should be willing to research and find out. They should always read the bills. Always. And that is not happening now. People are just voting for a stupid freedom score without ever reading a bill. Yeah. It's crazy. I'm amazed how bad it has gotten over the years. Have you seen a change in, you know, Idaho? Uh, Randy staples, his book, uh, Paradox Politics is whenever he points you to. But I think it's pretty well agreed upon that Idaho has had a history of being kind of this, uh, not radical center, but like, state over party. So they're gonna get from Borah to McClure. Uh, Andrus, you have these figures that are much more interested, I think, in positioning the state and dealing with the issues that the state has as opposed to federal politics. Um, have you seen a change? Does that seem like it's the case? Because, Wolf, reintroduction to me seemed like maybe that's a, you know, this kind of changeover and it's not necessarily the state behaving that way. It's federal politics are taking center stage as opposed to local issues. And it's kind of this push and pull. So I went off on a Jag, but. We always push and pull with the federal government. You know, I'm the chairman of our federalism committee in the House, and we started that about six years ago, I guess, so that we could say to the federal government, hey, you know, stay in your lane. Some of this stuff is none of your business. So butt out. Are there. Have there been efforts? You know, the Sagebrush Rebellion, I think was the first example I can think of where there were a state. I think it was Nevada willing to put forward legislation, I think at the local level, but just about public lands or something. Is there have there been other attempts at the Idaho state level to push back against federal regulation and try to wrestle back some, some? Oh, all the time. All the time. An example this year I used our constitutional police powers. So those are the powers for health and safety of the people. Mhm. And there's been numerous Supreme Court cases. In fact, the U.S. Supreme Court has never turned down the states when they use their police powers. So this was over wildfires because we're all sick of our land being burnt up and smelling smoke for months. Smoke is really bad for our health. Really, really bad. A lot worse than smoking cigarettes. Mhm. So our federalism committee had a whole day session on wildfire and I entitled it, uh, federal land management by incineration. And so we had foresters come speak. Um, we had the policy center from U of I come and talk to us. We had our our Legislative Council talk about the police powers. We had a former Forest Service person come and explain what the big box burning is. They just draw a line and they ignite the fire, and everything there burns. Doesn't matter if it's private or state or federal. So I took that and I wrote a bill and passed it, and the governor signed it. So from now on, if there is a lightning strike, instead of waiting until it's 10,000 acres before the federal government begins to put it out, he can declare an emergency and he can send any state resources. So Department of Lands or even the rangeland fire protection districts, the local fire departments, whomever is close a logger can go put out the lightning strike tree instead of igniting the whole country. Well, it's interesting because my the way I've thought about wolf reintroduction has been, you know, this obviously, this push and pull between the states and the and the federal government, such as, you know, barring the state Fish and Wildlife from being involved, that's something that the state level they can do. Um, so I'll merge this with my sausage being made. Or what have you learned about how policy is formed? Um, you know, have those attempts, uh, you know, to, to exercise your police powers or to exercise, you know, the states. I'm from Texas. And they did, you know, something similar with the border and, uh, recently, um, are there things like that around wolf reintroduction, aside from maybe stopping the, the fish and wildlife that you can think of or, or were there, was there a sense that we need to do something to push back against D.C.? And. Well, we did quite a few things. We, you know, put things into law so that it would allow our ranchers to go ahead and kill wolves before they weren't even allowed to kill them if they were threatened. If a human was threatened, they could shoot the wolf. But that was it. They were supposed to stand there and watch their animals be destroyed, torn to pieces. So we looked at the legislation or we looked at the agreement where it said, you know, about worrying, harassing if a wolf is worrying and harassing. That was in the federal, the federal settlement, the federal agreement. So I said, all right, we're going to make that a state law. So if a rancher sees a wolf who is lying, we use their exact language, lying in wait, worrying, harassing. Then you can kill the animal and you're not thrown in jail. And this was what, year? Oh, gosh. Was this. After de-listing? Oh, yeah. Okay. I can't remember what. I guess it would. Have to be. Exactly. That was because they were trying to say no fish and game. You can only go kill one animal out of this whole pack. All that does is this smarten up the pack. Right. So I've heard. So it gave the ranchers at least the ability to do something. And then the next thing was, okay, now you can have an agent, you can name anybody you want. And as many people as you want to be your agent. And if they see wolves near your cattle, they can kill them. Okay. And we just took one step at a time, you know, eating the elephant right at a time. Well, I guess that gets around to my second question about policy. So in your experience, I mean, is this a lot of back, you know, table lunch we're having, you know, midnight conversations is it is the influence of the public more important than the feelings of the legislator? Uh, how does you know a piece of legislation go from, you will do this to actually being implemented in your experience? Um, you know, sometimes constituents bring you ideas and so you can figure out how to make them fit into law. The most important thing about getting a bill passed is the relationship you have with your colleagues, because you have to have 36 votes in the House. You know, you've got to have 18 in the Senate, and you have to have the governor sign it. So you have to have good relationships in all three places. And if you have a bad relationship with the governor, well, then you find his friends to have good relationship, that they can go and talk to him. So I had a complete rewrite of our private property rights, our trespass bill, and the governor was mad at me. This was Otter. He was mad at me for something that happened years and years before. And so he it was the last piece of legislation in his term. Then he was done. Then as governor, and he wanted to veto it because it was mine. But I had gathered this coalition of all the ranchers, the Farm Bureau, the utility companies, ayaki everybody. And we had pushed this through with huge numbers. So it was veto proof. Mhm. And then I had his former chief of staff go talk to him. And so he couldn't veto it. Mm. That's. Well to, to finish it off, do you have any closing comments about uh both your political career. But then also I guess natural resources and natural resource issues in Idaho. Well, the best part about this job as a legislator is you get to help people. If they're having trouble with an agency, you can pick up the phone and call the director and say, can you explain to me why one of your people said no to this? Or making this person jump through hoops that I don't see in the rules? And then the director said, I had no idea they were doing that. We will straighten this out. Um, so that's enjoyable. And then passing bills that are going to help your constituents, that's what you're there for. You're not there to blow up things. You're there to help your constituents always, and to be a statesman and not a politician. That's very important. Well, Representative Boyle, thank you for spending the time with me today. Thank you. To talk a little bit, uh, I hope we get to sit down again and I will have have more pointed questions, I'm sure. Uh, as we get into the actual writing of. I'm only on the first chapter, so I have yet to really get into the the meat and potatoes of it. But, uh, hopefully I'll talk to you later this summer. I would love that. Thank you. I would love.

lines.line: > The speaker introduces himself as Stephen F. Austin, a doctoral candidate at the University of Idaho, conducting an oral history interview with Representative Judy Boyle. The interview is part of a project supported by the Idaho Historical Society, focusing on wolf reintroduction in Idaho and its political implications. The speaker outlines the purpose of the interview and the broader commemorative mission of the project.

metadata.title: Introduction to the Interview
metadata.synopsis: The speaker introduces himself as Stephen F. Austin, a doctoral candidate at the University of Idaho, conducting an oral history interview with Representative Judy Boyle. The interview is part of a project supported by the Idaho Historical Society, focusing on wolf reintroduction in Idaho and its political implications. The speaker outlines the purpose of the interview and the broader commemorative mission of the project.

lines.line: > Judy Boyle recounts her early exposure to politics, which began with her grandfather taking her to public meetings. She shares a memorable incident from her childhood that shaped her understanding of honesty and integrity in public dealings. This early influence deeply impacted her perspective on public service and her eventual political career.

metadata.title: Early Influences in Politics
metadata.synopsis: Judy Boyle recounts her early exposure to politics, which began with her grandfather taking her to public meetings. She shares a memorable incident from her childhood that shaped her understanding of honesty and integrity in public dealings. This early influence deeply impacted her perspective on public service and her eventual political career.

lines.line: # Transition to Idaho and Educational Background

> Judy Boyle discusses her educational journey, starting in California and moving through community college. Her family's decision to relocate to Idaho due to changes in their local community, including the establishment of prisons, is highlighted. This move was pivotal in shaping her future in Idaho politics.

metadata.title: Transition to Idaho and Educational Background
metadata.synopsis: Judy Boyle discusses her educational journey, starting in California and moving through community college. Her family's decision to relocate to Idaho due to changes in their local community, including the establishment of prisons, is highlighted. This move was pivotal in shaping her future in Idaho politics.

lines.line: > Judy Boyle details her immediate involvement in Idaho politics upon moving to the state, including attending various meetings. She describes how she met Helen Chenoweth, a like-minded politician, and began working with her. Their shared views on environmental politics and natural resource management significantly influenced their political activities and objectives.

metadata.title: Professional Relationship with Helen Chenoweth
metadata.synopsis: Judy Boyle details her immediate involvement in Idaho politics upon moving to the state, including attending various meetings. She describes how she met Helen Chenoweth, a like-minded politician, and began working with her. Their shared views on environmental politics and natural resource management significantly influenced their political activities and objectives.

lines.line: > The speaker discusses the contentious issue of wolf reintroduction in Idaho, which began in the late 1980s. She describes the widespread opposition from various community and political groups, the federal government's determination to proceed, and the significant impact on local wildlife and ranching. This issue became a major point of contention and shaped much of her subsequent political focus.

metadata.title: Wolf Reintroduction Controversy
metadata.synopsis: The speaker discusses the contentious issue of wolf reintroduction in Idaho, which began in the late 1980s. She describes the widespread opposition from various community and political groups, the federal government's determination to proceed, and the significant impact on local wildlife and ranching. This issue became a major point of contention and shaped much of her subsequent political focus.

lines.line: > Judy Boyle reflects on her legislative career, which began in 2009 after being encouraged by her peers. She emphasizes the importance of helping constituents and maintaining good relationships with colleagues to effectively pass legislation. Her approach to public service is characterized by a commitment to her constituents and a focus on practical outcomes rather than political gamesmanship.

metadata.title: Legislative Career and Public Service
metadata.synopsis: Judy Boyle reflects on her legislative career, which began in 2009 after being encouraged by her peers. She emphasizes the importance of helping constituents and maintaining good relationships with colleagues to effectively pass legislation. Her approach to public service is characterized by a commitment to her constituents and a focus on practical outcomes rather than political gamesmanship.

# Transition to Idaho and Educational Background

> Judy Boyle discusses her educational journey, starting in California and moving through community college. > Judy Boyle reflects on her legislative career, which began in 2009 after being encouraged by her peers. She emphasizes the importance of helping constituents and maintaining good relationships with colleagues to effectively pass legislation. Her approach to public service is characterized by a commitment to her constituents and a focus on practical outcomes rather than political gamesmanship.

story.title: Judy Boyle- Wolf Reintroduction May 14, 2024
story.description: The transcript details an interview with Representative Judy Boyle, primarily focusing on her political career and the wolf reintroduction issue in Idaho. This discussion, contextualized within the broader political and environmental debates in Idaho, covers the challenges and opposition faced during the federal wolf reintroduction program initiated in the 1990s. The conversation highlights the state-federal tensions, with residents opposing what they saw as federal overreach. Boyle shares her experiences with natural resource policies and describes legislative strategies undertaken to mitigate the impact on local agriculture and livestock. The interview concludes with reflections on public service and state sovereignty in the face of federal interventions.
